Chapter 4: Quantitative Causal Models

Conditional probability tables, Bayes’ rule, and Bayesian networks

Learning Objectives

After this chapter, you will be able to:

  • Define probability distributions over discrete variable states and apply the basic axioms
  • Compute conditional probabilities and construct Conditional Probability Tables (CPTs)
  • Apply Bayes’ rule to update beliefs when new evidence arrives
  • Explain the Causal Markov Condition and how a DAG factorizes a joint distribution
  • Build a quantitative Bayesian network in R using bnlearn and query it for MNH policy decisions

Key Concepts

Conditional Probability Tables (CPTs)

A CPT lists P(Child | Parents) for every combination of parent and child states. Each row in the table defines a conditional distribution, and every row must sum to 1. CPTs are the quantitative building blocks of a Bayesian network – they encode the strength of causal relationships that qualitative models could only describe with [+] and [-] signs. In practice, CPTs are estimated from facility-level data, expert elicitation, or both.

Bayes’ Rule

Bayes’ rule lets us “flip” the conditioning direction: given P(Evidence | Cause), it computes P(Cause | Evidence). The formula is P(A | B) = P(B | A) x P(A) / P(B). The prior P(A) represents what we believed before seeing evidence, the likelihood P(B | A) represents how probable the evidence is if the hypothesis is true, and the posterior P(A | B) represents our updated belief after incorporating evidence. This is the fundamental operation of situational assessment – reasoning backward from what we observe to what is likely true.

The Causal Markov Condition

Each variable in a DAG is conditionally independent of its non-descendants given its parents. This means the full joint distribution factorizes as a product of CPTs: P(X1, X2, …, Xn) = Product of P(Xi | Parents(Xi)). This factorization reduces computational complexity from exponential to manageable – instead of specifying millions of probabilities, the causal structure lets us specify just a few hundred.

Bayesian Network

A Bayesian network equals a DAG plus a CPT for every node. It allows us to compute exact probabilities for any query, update beliefs with Bayes’ rule when evidence arrives, and compare interventions using the do-operator. The do-operator severs incoming arrows to the intervention variable and sets it to a fixed value, giving us the causal effect rather than a mere correlation.

Lecture Slides

Open slides full-screen →

R Code Workshop

This chapter builds a complete Bayesian network in R using bnlearn, specifies CPTs for each node, and demonstrates forward queries, interventional queries, and sensitivity analysis.

Define the Network Structure

We use bnlearn’s model string syntax to specify the DAG. The notation [Node|Parent1:Parent2] means a node is conditional on its parents, while [Node] alone denotes a root node with no parents.

library(bnlearn)

# Define the DAG structure using bnlearn's model string syntax
# [Node] means no parents; [Node|Parent1:Parent2] means conditional on parents
dag <- model2network(
  "[Staffing][Equipment][Quality|Staffing:Equipment][NMR|Quality]"
)

# Inspect the structure
dag
nodes(dag)    # "Staffing" "Equipment" "Quality" "NMR"
arcs(dag)     # Shows the 3 directed edges

Specify CPTs

Each CPT is defined as an array with named dimensions. Root nodes get simple marginal distributions, while child nodes get conditional distributions indexed by all parent state combinations.

# Define CPTs using arrays (states must match exactly)
cpt_staffing <- array(c(0.40, 0.60), dim = 2,
  dimnames = list(Staffing = c("Adequate", "Low")))

cpt_equipment <- array(c(0.35, 0.65), dim = 2,
  dimnames = list(Equipment = c("Available", "Unavailable")))

cpt_quality <- array(
  c(0.85, 0.15,  # Adequate + Available
    0.50, 0.50,  # Low + Available
    0.55, 0.45,  # Adequate + Unavailable
    0.20, 0.80), # Low + Unavailable
  dim = c(2, 2, 2),
  dimnames = list(
    Quality   = c("Good", "Poor"),
    Staffing  = c("Adequate", "Low"),
    Equipment = c("Available", "Unavailable")))

cpt_nmr <- array(
  c(0.85, 0.15,  # Good quality
    0.40, 0.60), # Poor quality
  dim = c(2, 2),
  dimnames = list(NMR = c("Low", "High"), Quality = c("Good", "Poor")))

# Combine into a fitted BN
bn_mnh <- custom.fit(dag, list(
  Staffing = cpt_staffing, Equipment = cpt_equipment,
  Quality = cpt_quality, NMR = cpt_nmr))

Query the Network

The cpquery() function uses likelihood weighting to estimate conditional probabilities from the network. For exact inference, we convert the network to a junction tree using the gRain package.

# Query: P(NMR = High | Staffing = Low)
cpquery(bn_mnh,
  event  = (NMR == "High"),
  evidence = (Staffing == "Low"),
  n = 1e6)
# Expected: ~0.44

# Query: P(NMR = High | Staffing = Adequate, Equipment = Available)
cpquery(bn_mnh,
  event  = (NMR == "High"),
  evidence = (Staffing == "Adequate" & Equipment == "Available"),
  n = 1e6)
# Expected: ~0.22

# Exact inference using grain (junction tree)
library(gRain)
bn_grain <- as.grain(bn_mnh)
querygrain(bn_grain, nodes = "NMR")
# Returns marginal P(NMR) across all facilities

Visualize the Network

Two visualization options are available: bnlearn’s built-in plot using Rgraphviz, and ggdag for publication-quality figures built on ggplot2.

# Option 1: bnlearn's built-in plot (uses Rgraphviz)
graphviz.plot(bn_mnh, main = "MNH Bayesian Network",
  shape = "rectangle",
  highlight = list(nodes = "NMR", col = "#E24A3F", fill = "#E24A3F"))

# Option 2: ggdag for publication-quality figures
library(ggdag)
library(dagitty)

mnh_dag <- dagitty('dag {
  Staffing [pos="0,0"]
  Equipment [pos="2,0"]
  Quality [pos="1,1"]
  NMR [pos="1,2"]
  Staffing -> Quality
  Equipment -> Quality
  Quality -> NMR
}')

ggdag(mnh_dag) +
  theme_dag() +
  labs(title = "MNH Causal Model (4 Nodes)")

Interventional Queries

The mutilated() function implements the do-operator – it severs incoming arrows to the intervention variable and sets it to a fixed value. This lets us compare the causal effect of different interventions side by side.

# Simulate do(Staffing = Adequate) by mutilating the network
bn_do_adequate <- mutilated(bn_mnh, evidence = list(Staffing = "Adequate"))

# P(NMR | do(Staffing = Adequate))
querygrain(as.grain(bn_do_adequate), nodes = "NMR")

# Compare scenarios
scenarios <- data.frame(
  Scenario = c("Status Quo", "do(Staff=Adequate)",
               "do(Equip=Available)", "do(Both)"),
  P_NMR_High = c(
    querygrain(as.grain(bn_mnh),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"],
    querygrain(as.grain(
      mutilated(bn_mnh, list(Staffing = "Adequate"))),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"],
    querygrain(as.grain(
      mutilated(bn_mnh, list(Equipment = "Available"))),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"],
    querygrain(as.grain(
      mutilated(bn_mnh, list(Staffing = "Adequate",
                              Equipment = "Available"))),
               nodes = "NMR")$NMR["High"]))

# Display comparison
scenarios$Reduction <- scenarios$P_NMR_High[1] - scenarios$P_NMR_High
print(scenarios)

Key Takeaways

Quantitative causal models = DAG + CPTs = Bayesian Network. They allow us to compute exact probabilities, update beliefs with Bayes’ rule, and compare interventions using the do-operator.

Probability fundamentals: States must be MECE; probabilities must sum to 1. CPTs encode the quantitative relationships in the DAG.

Bayes’ rule lets us reason backward from evidence to causes – essential for situational assessment.

The Causal Markov Condition simplifies computation from exponential to manageable. Sensitivity analysis reveals which interventions have the largest expected impact – in the MNH model, staffing has a larger marginal effect than equipment, but the two are synergistic.

Looking Ahead

Next in Chapter 5 – Situational Analysis, we turn to the first major application of quantitative causal models:

  • Updating beliefs with multiple evidence streams – combining facility data, surveys, and expert judgment
  • Diagnostic inference – given observed outcomes, which upstream causes are most likely?
  • The explaining away phenomenon – how one piece of evidence changes the relevance of another
  • A complete district diagnosis using Bayesian inference on the MNH causal model
